summary: Gruber responds to Mark Gurman's Bloomberg report on the shaky debut of Apple's Catalyst technology for bringing iPad apps to the Mac. He pushes back on the framing that paying separately for iPad and Mac versions is a Catalyst problem, noting this has always been the case. He highlights the extensive technical issues developers are encountering, including touch-centric UI elements appearing on Mac, cursor and scrolling bugs, and performance problems. He notes that even Apple's own showcase app Asphalt 9 was pulled before launch despite being demoed four months earlier, and that Netflix has declined to participate entirely.
key_points: The expectation of paying only once for both iPad and Mac versions actually makes it harder for developers to justify building Mac apps at all
Catalyst's technical problems are extensive — from touch UI paradigms leaking onto Mac to video, scrolling, and performance issues
Apple's own WWDC showcase app Asphalt 9 was pulled before launch, undermining Catalyst's credibility for games
Netflix categorically declined to use Catalyst despite being a top iPad app, and Gruber notes they could have built a native Mac app long ago if they wanted one
Catalyst is a developer technology that users shouldn't need to know about — the real issue is whether it produces good Mac apps
money_quotes: "You have to pay for iPad and Mac versions separately" doesn't seem like a big deal to me because it's been that way all along, regardless of Catalyst.
Worse, the expectation that you should pay only once for both iPad and Mac versions of an app makes it hard for developers of commercial software to justify doing a Mac app, period.
The rest of Gurman's article is about how much work it takes to create a good Mac app even with Catalyst.
Other than that, how do you like the APIs, Mrs. Lincoln?
Maybe it's not so easy, and maybe Catalyst is not good for games.
I'm not sure what Netflix's calculus is here, but the simple truth is that if Netflix wanted a native Mac app they would have made one long ago.
key_insight: Catalyst's debut reveals a tension between Apple's promise of easy iPad-to-Mac porting and the reality that making a good Mac app still requires substantial platform-specific work, regardless of the tools.
